quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Elastic collision"?

options.text: ω = ω₀ + αt, θ = θ₀ + ω₀t + ½αt², ω² = ω₀² + 2α(θ-θ₀); for constant α
A×B; a vector perpendicular to both A and B with magnitude ABsinθ; used in calculating torque and magnetic force
ω = ω₀ + αt, θ = θ₀ + ω₀t + ½αt², ω² = ω₀² + 2α(θ - θ₀) for constant angular acceleration
A collision in which both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved; objects bounce apart with no KE loss

quiz_questions.explanation: "Elastic collision" is defined as: A collision in which both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved; objects bounce apart with no KE loss. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Perfectly inelastic collision"?

options.text: A collision in which the objects stick together; maximum kinetic energy is lost while momentum is still conserved
F_net = ma or F_net = dp/dt; the net force equals mass times acceleration, or equivalently the rate of change of momentum
F_c = mv²/r = mrω²; the net radial force required to maintain circular motion at a given radius and speed
v(t) = dx/dt; the derivative of position with respect to time, giving the rate and direction of motion at each instant

quiz_questions.explanation: "Perfectly inelastic collision" is defined as: A collision in which the objects stick together; maximum kinetic energy is lost while momentum is still conserved. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Coefficient of restitution"?

options.text: A force whose work depends only on initial and final positions, not the path taken (e.g., gravity, spring force); associated with potential energy
e = relative speed after / relative speed before collision; e = 1 (elastic), 0 < e < 1 (partially inelastic), e = 0 (perfectly inelastic)
A mass on a string tracing a horizontal circle; T sinθ provides centripetal force while T cosθ balances gravity
When two waves overlap, the resultant displacement is the sum of individual displacements; leads to interference and standing waves

quiz_questions.explanation: "Coefficient of restitution" is defined as: e = relative speed after / relative speed before collision; e = 1 (elastic), 0 < e < 1 (partially inelastic), e = 0 (perfectly inelastic). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "1D elastic collision formulas"?

options.text: F_ext,net = M_total × a_cm; the center of mass accelerates as if all mass and net external force were concentrated there
F_ext = dp/dt applied to rockets: thrust = v_exhaust × |dm/dt|, where mass decreases as fuel is expelled
v₁' = ((m₁-m₂)/(m₁+m₂))v₁ + (2m₂/(m₁+m₂))v₂; derived from simultaneous momentum and KE conservation
f_s ≤ μ_s N; opposes the tendency of motion between surfaces in contact; self-adjusting up to the maximum value μ_s N

quiz_questions.explanation: "1D elastic collision formulas" is defined as: v₁' = ((m₁-m₂)/(m₁+m₂))v₁ + (2m₂/(m₁+m₂))v₂; derived from simultaneous momentum and KE conservation. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Energy loss in collisions"?

options.text: E = KE + PE = -GMm/(2r); negative for bound orbits; more negative means a lower, tighter orbit
ΔKE = KE_f - KE_i; negative for inelastic collisions; equals -½μv_rel² where μ = m₁m₂/(m₁+m₂) is the reduced mass
1 hp = 746 W; a unit of power commonly used for engines and motors
An inclined road surface where the normal force has a radial component, providing centripetal force without relying solely on friction

quiz_questions.explanation: "Energy loss in collisions" is defined as: ΔKE = KE_f - KE_i; negative for inelastic collisions; equals -½μv_rel² where μ = m₁m₂/(m₁+m₂) is the reduced mass. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A collision in which both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved; objects bounce apart with no KE loss"?

options.text: Elastic collision
Rotational kinetic energy
Work-Energy Theorem
Total orbital energy

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Elastic collision".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A collision in which the objects stick together; maximum kinetic energy is lost while momentum is still conserved"?

options.text: Velocity of center of mass
Impulse approximation
Perfectly inelastic collision
Superposition

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Perfectly inelastic collision".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "e = relative speed after / relative speed before collision; e = 1 (elastic), 0 < e < 1 (partially inelastic), e = 0 (perfectly inelastic)"?

options.text: Angular position (θ)
Non-uniform circular motion
Velocity and acceleration in SHM
Coefficient of restitution

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Coefficient of restitution".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "v₁' = ((m₁-m₂)/(m₁+m₂))v₁ + (2m₂/(m₁+m₂))v₂; derived from simultaneous momentum and KE conservation"?

options.text: Center of mass (discrete)
Free-body diagram
Torque
1D elastic collision formulas

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "1D elastic collision formulas".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "ΔKE = KE_f - KE_i; negative for inelastic collisions; equals -½μv_rel² where μ = m₁m₂/(m₁+m₂) is the reduced mass"?

options.text: Binding energy
Unit vector
Energy loss in collisions
Energy dissipation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Energy loss in collisions".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Collisions is accurate?

options.text: Dynamics problems in AP Physics C require setting up and solving differential equations when forces depend on velocity or position.
Collisions are classified by whether kinetic energy is conserved.
Angular Kinematics describes rotational motion using angular position θ(t), angular velocity ω(t) = dθ/dt, and angular acceleration α(t) = dω/dt.
Work and Energy provide a scalar framework for analyzing motion without tracking forces and accelerations over time.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Collisions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Collisions?

options.text: Gravitational Potential Energy in the universal form is U = -GMm/r, where r is the distance between centers of mass.
Orbital Motion applies Newton's Second Law with gravitational force to derive orbital parameters.
The critical condition at the top of a vertical loop (minimum speed for maintaining contact) occurs when the normal force equals zero: mg = mv²/r, giving v_min = √(gr).
In elastic collisions, both momentum and KE are conserved, yielding two equations to solve for two unknowns.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Collisions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Collisions?

options.text: The parallel axis theorem I = I_cm + Md² relates the moment of inertia about any axis to that about the center of mass.
In perfectly inelastic collisions, the objects stick together and maximum KE is lost (converted to thermal/sound energy).
Dynamics connects force and motion through Newton's three laws.
Systematic problem-solving involves drawing free-body diagrams, choosing a coordinate system, applying ΣF = ma in each direction, and solving the resulting system of equations.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Collisions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Collisions?

options.text: Force-potential energy diagrams reveal equilibrium positions (where F = -dU/dx = 0) and their stability (slope determines restoring or repelling character).
SHM is the foundation for understanding waves, circuits, and quantum mechanics.
Gravitational Potential Energy in the universal form is U = -GMm/r, where r is the distance between centers of mass.
Real collisions are partially inelastic.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Collisions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Collisions?

options.text: SHM is the foundation for understanding waves, circuits, and quantum mechanics.
The coefficient of restitution e = (v₂' - v₁')/(v₁ - v₂) quantifies the elasticity of a collision (e = 1 elastic, e = 0 perfectly inelastic).
Efficiency η = useful energy output / total energy input ranges from 0 to 1 and is limited by friction, heat losses, and the laws of thermodynamics.
Free-body diagrams identify all forces acting on an object, enabling systematic application of F = ma in each coordinate direction.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Collisions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Collisions?

options.text: Automotive airbag design increasing collision time to reduce peak force on occupants while the same impulse (momentum change) occurs
Calculating the power required for an elevator motor to lift a loaded car at constant speed: P = mgv
Spacecraft trajectory planning using Newton's Second Law with thrust force, gravitational force, and changing mass to compute acceleration profiles
Vehicle accident reconstruction using partially inelastic collision models and coefficient of restitution to determine pre-collision speeds

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Collisions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Collisions?

options.text: Designing anti-lock braking systems (ABS) that maintain static friction (which exceeds kinetic) by preventing wheel lockup during emergency stops
Neutron moderation in nuclear reactors: elastic collisions between neutrons and hydrogen atoms (equal mass) transfer maximum kinetic energy in a single collision
Athletic performance analysis measuring power output of cyclists and runners to optimize training and predict race performance
Communication satellite placement in geosynchronous orbit at r ≈ 42,200 km ensuring 24-hour period for continuous coverage of a fixed ground region

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Collisions.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Collisions applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Sports equipment design (golf clubs, tennis rackets) optimizing mass ratios and coefficients of restitution for maximum energy transfer to the ball
Automobile engine specifications using power curves (horsepower vs. RPM) to match engine performance to vehicle weight and desired acceleration
GPS satellite corrections accounting for the variation of g with altitude to maintain precise timing and positioning
Designing roller coasters using calculus-based kinematics to ensure smooth transitions between acceleration phases and safe g-force limits

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Collisions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Collisions?

options.text: Musical instrument design: string tension and density determine wave speed and thus the fundamental frequency of vibrating strings
Calculating the work done by a non-constant wind force on a sail using numerical integration of force-displacement data
Flywheel energy storage systems using high-I, high-ω wheels to store kinetic energy for regenerative braking in vehicles and power grid stabilization
Vehicle accident reconstruction using partially inelastic collision models and coefficient of restitution to determine pre-collision speeds

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Collisions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Collisions in practice?

options.text: Ice skater spins: decreasing I by pulling arms in causes ω to increase to conserve L; extending arms slows the spin
Wind turbine performance analysis relating angular velocity to blade tip speed and power output at different wind conditions
Neutron moderation in nuclear reactors: elastic collisions between neutrons and hydrogen atoms (equal mass) transfer maximum kinetic energy in a single collision
Sports biomechanics analyzing the impulse delivered by a bat to a baseball using force-time graphs from high-speed sensors

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Collisions.
